abstract:
The diffusion model is a powerful framework for inferring the latent psychological processes that drive decision-making and for linking these processes to neural mechanisms through response time data. While open-source software exists to fit these models, traditional estimation methods often require extensive data per subject to produce reliable results and typically only offer point estimates. In contrast, hierarchical Bayesian methods enhance statistical power by simultaneously estimating individual parameters and the group-level distributions from which they originate, while also providing a natural measure of parameter uncertainty via posterior distributions.

In this paper, we introduce HDDM (Hierarchical Drift Diffusion Model), a novel Python-based toolbox designed for the fast and flexible estimation of the drift-diffusion model and the related linear ballistic accumulator model. HDDM is particularly advantageous because it requires fewer trials per subject than non-hierarchical methods, supports robust Bayesian data analysis, and effectively handles outliers. Notably, the toolbox also enables researchers to estimate how trial-by-trial measurements—such as fMRI or EEG activity—influence specific decision-making parameters. We provide a theoretical overview of the drift-diffusion model and Bayesian inference, followed by a demonstration of the toolbox using a real-world dataset. Finally, through parameter recovery studies, we show that HDDM outperforms alternative fitting techniques, including maximum likelihood estimation and the χ²-quantile method.

The software and comprehensive documentation are available at: http://ski.clps.brown.edu/hddm_docs/
